text:
Introduction to
Generative AI
and LLMs
Dr. Tamer Arafa
1

What is generative AI
●
Generative AI represents a major shift in artificial intelligence—from analysis to creation. These
systems don’t just interpret information; they generate new possibilities.
●
By training on massive datasets, they learn underlying structures of language, imagery, and
sound, allowing them to produce highly realistic and useful content.
●
This capability extends across industries—from personalized marketing and design to scientific
discovery—fundamentally changing how we create and innovate.
2

Language AI
&
Language Models
7

What Is Language AI?
●
Language AI refers to a subfield of AI that focuses on developing technologies capable of
understanding, processing, and generating human language.
●
The term Language AI can often be used interchangeably with natural language processing
(NLP)
8

The History of Language AI
9

Language AI Capabilities
10

The language modeling problem
A language model answers the question: What is p(text)?
Text is a sequence of symbols:
Just the chain rule of
probability– no simplifying
assumptions!
context
11

The language modeling problem
[the rest of the vocabulary]
context
12

How do Language Models Work
●
Next-word prediction: Language models estimate the probability of the next word given previous
words in a sequence.
●
Markov assumption: They simplify this by assuming each word depends only on a limited
number of previous words (e.g., the last one or few), not the entire history.
●
Learning from data: The model learns these conditional probabilities from large text datasets
and uses them to generate or evaluate new text sequences
13

Markov assumption
●
We make the Markov assumption: x(t+1) depends only on the preceding n-1 words
assumption
n-1 words
14

Markov assumption
●
P(the | its water is so transparent that) ≅P(the | transparent that)
or maybe even
●
P(the | its water is so transparent that) ≅P(the | that)
15

n-gramLanguageModels
●
“I have a dog whose name is Lucy. I have two cats, they like playing with Lucy.”
●
Question: How to teach a Language Model?
●
Answer (pre- Deep Learning): teach an n-gram Language Model!
●
Idea: Collect statistics about how frequent different n-grams are and use these to predict next word
16

20

Large Language Models (LLMs)
●
The transformer model allows fast parallel computations on many GPUs (large amounts of
compute)
●
It allows training on large amounts of data (think the whole internet worth of text).
●
It allows adding many and many layers in the model (large model)
●
A large language model is a language model with a large number of parameters, trained on large
amounts of data, for long period of time.
21

Neural language models
f(Θ)
I have a dog whose name is Lucy. I have two
A differentiable
function (e.g. a
neural network)
cat
A distribution over
the vocabulary
22

Neural language models
●
Vocab ( ): {dogs, cats, bananas}
●
Model logits (from 𝑓Θ ) : [2.1, 1.3, −0.5]
●
Apply softmax →probabilities:
○
dogs: 𝑒2.1/ σ ≈8.17/12.44 ≈0.66
○
cats: 𝑒1.3/ σ ≈3.67/12.44 ≈0.30
○
bananas: 𝑒−0.5/ σ ≈0.61/12.44 ≈0.05
●
Generation: Greedy pick →“dogs”.
●
Backprop updates Θ to make “dogs” even more likely next time in similar contexts.
23

How do we maximize the likelihood?
●
The dominant strategy from the past decade:
1.
The randomly initialized differentiable function (neural network) takes the context as input
2.
Have that function output a probability distribution over the vocabulary
3.
Treat the probability of the correct token as your objective to maximize.
4.
Or negative log (probability) as your objective to minimize
5.
Differentiate with respect to the parameters, and perform gradient descent
24

Gradient Descent
●
Q: Given current parameter w, should we make w bigger or smaller to minimize our loss?
●
A: Move w in the reverse direction from the slope of the function
25

Large Language Models
26

●
LLM is a type of transformer model
○
Transformer:
●
A neural network that learns context
and meaning by tracking relationships
in sequential data
LLM Architecture
27

Transformer Architecture
Encoder
Encodes inputs (“prompts”) with
contextual understanding and
produces one vector per input
token.
Decoder
Accepts input tokens
and generates new
tokens.
28

Transformer Architecture
29

Transformer Architecture
●
Encoder-decoder architecture
●
Encoder extracts features from an input sequence
●
Decoder uses the features to produce an output
sentence
Hur mår du?
How are you?
Case: translation
(output)
TRANSFORMER
30

Encoder Only LLMs
●
Suited for tasks that can understand language,
●
such as toxicity classification and sentiment analysis.
Example: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)
Sentiment analysis of tweets
31

Decoder Only LLMs
●
Suited for generating language and content,
●
such as story writing, blog generation, open-domain Q/A and virtual assistants.
ChatBots
Example: GPT-3 (Generative Pretrained Transformer 3)
32

Encoder Decoder LLMs
●
Combine the encoder and decoder components of the transformer architecture
●
Suited for both understanding and generating content,
●
such as translation, text-to-code and summarisations.
●
Example: T5 (Text-to-Text Transformers) by HuggingFace
33

Multimodal LLMs
●
Unimodal LLMs
○
Trained on a single modality of data, such as text
○
Lack visual context
●
Multimodal LLMs:
○
Understand and generate content across multiple modalities,
○
such as text, images, audio, video, or other sensor data
●
Vision-language models:
○
Multimodal models that can learn from images and text
34

Visual Language Models
●
Main idea: Unify the image and text representation,
and feed it to a textual decoder for generation
●
Typically consists of two main components:
○
Vision encoder: extracts image features and
encodes them into a format that can be
understood by the language decoder.
Example: ResNet, ViT
○
Language decoder: takes these encoded
visual features along with any textual input
and generates descriptions, or captions, etc.
Example: GPT-2, GPT-3
35

VLMs
36

Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP)
●
CLIP differs from traditional vision-language models
○
It does not generate text descriptions or captions for images
○
Focuses on learning a joint representation space where images and text can be
compared directly
●
Enables various downstream tasks, such as
○
Zero-shot image classification: classify images into one of several classes, without
any prior training or knowledge of the classes
○
Zero-shot image retrieval, text-based image generation
37

Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP)
•
Jointly trains a text encoder and an image encoder to predict the correct image—text pair
Contrastive Pre-training
Radford, Alec, et al. "Learning transferable visual models from natural language supervision." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2021.
Text embeddings
Transformer
(ResNet or ViT)
Image embeddings
Contrastive learning framework
•
Maximize the cosine similarities between
correct image-text pairs
•
Minimize the cosine similarities for
dissimilar pairs (non-diagonal elements)
